question: Define an open ball and a neighborhood (or open set) in a metric space (X, d). Using the definition, prove that every open ball is a neighborhood of its center.
expectedPoints: State the definition of an open ball B(x₀; r) = { x ∈ X | d(x, x₀) < r }.
State the definition of a neighborhood of a point x: a set N that contains an open ball centered at x. That is, there exists an ε > 0 such that B(x; ε) ⊆ N.
For a given open ball B(a; r), show that it is a neighborhood of its center 'a'. Choose ε = r, then B(a; r) ⊆ B(a; r) is trivially true, fulfilling the definition.

question: Let (X, d) be a metric space. Prove that a set G ⊆ X is open if and only if it is a neighborhood of each of its points.
expectedPoints: (⇒) Assume G is open. Take any point x ∈ G. Since G is open, there exists an r > 0 such that B(x; r) ⊆ G. This is precisely the definition of G being a neighborhood of x.
(⇐) Assume G is a neighborhood of each of its points. This means for every x ∈ G, there exists an r_x > 0 such that B(x; r_x) ⊆ G.
Show that G is equal to the union of all these open balls: G = ∪_{x∈G} B(x; r_x). Since each ball is open and a union of open sets is open, G must be open.

question: Consider the metric space (R², d) where d is the standard Euclidean metric. Let A = {(x, y) | x > 0 and y > 0} be the open first quadrant. Show that A is open by rigorously proving it is a neighborhood of every point within it. Discuss how your proof relies on the foundational properties of metric spaces and neighborhoods.
expectedPoints: Choose an arbitrary point (a, b) ∈ A. Therefore, a > 0 and b > 0.
Find a specific radius r (e.g., r = min(a, b)/2) such that the open ball B((a, b); r) is entirely contained within A. Justify this choice geometrically and algebraically.
Argue that for any (x, y) in this ball, the distance condition d((x,y), (a,b)) < r implies |x - a| < r and |y - b| < r. Use this to show x > 0 and y > 0, proving (x,y) ∈ A.

Generalize the argument: The proof works because the Euclidean metric allows us to measure distance in all directions, and the property of openness is defined through these distances (open balls). The proof is a concrete application of the general theorem that a set is open iff it is a neighborhood of each of its points.
